q: You need rows from a Delta table as they existed 7 days ago for a regulatory audit. But a colleague ran VACUUM with the retention set to 0 hours yesterday. What are your options?
a: Time travel is gone — VACUUM with a 0-hour retention deletes all data files not referenced by the current table version. Delta's transaction log entries (in _delta_log/) still exist but they point to Parquet files that have been deleted. Your options: (1) Restore from a backup snapshot if your organization takes regular Delta table snapshots or uses Databricks' managed backup; (2) Check Unity Catalog DESCRIBE HISTORY — if any older checkpoint or log file references external storage you control, data may still be there; (3) Escalate to your data engineering team to pull from the upstream source (Kafka replay, S3 raw zone, change-data-capture log) and reprocess the 7-day window. For prevention: never set retentionDuration below 7 days (the default). Running VACUUM with 0-hour retention requires explicitly disabling the safety check (SET spark.databricks.delta.retentionDurationCheck.enabled = false) — this flag exists precisely to block accidental data loss. Production governance: set delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration = '30 days' on audit-critical tables, and require a ticket/approval before any VACUUM below 7 days. Databricks strongly recommends a minimum of 7 days retention.
fj: I'd run a time travel query with VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF to get the old rows.
fs: Time travel reads the old data files — but VACUUM with 0-hour retention deleted those files. The transaction log version entries exist but the underlying Parquet is gone. The correct response is: data is unrecoverable via time travel; escalate to backups and upstream source replay, then fix the retention policy.

code.label: What they ran — bypassing the safety check
lines: RETAIN 0 HOURS;  -- deletes all old Parquet files

code.label: Production governance — prevent this on audit tables
lines: -- require it as a guard in your runbook.
